question.text: A reverse mortgage borrower wishes to prepay the entire outstanding loan amount during the loan tenure. Which of the following is correct?

answer.text: Prepayment is not allowed during the loan tenure
Prepayment is allowed but attracts a 2% penalty on the outstanding amount
Prepayment is allowed without any prepayment penalty
Prepayment is allowed only after 5 years of availing the loan

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Reverse mortgage loans carry NO prepayment penalty. The borrower (or their heirs) can repay the outstanding loan amount at any time without incurring any penalty charges. This provides flexibility if the borrower's financial circumstances change or if heirs wish to retain the property. **The Trap:** Many loan products carry prepayment penalties, so candidates may assume reverse mortgages do too. The "after 5 years" option borrows from the SGB early exit concept to create confusion. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-A covers loan features and prepayment terms. Prepayment penalty norms for various loan types are tested. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The no-prepayment-penalty feature is significant for heirs planning. If children come into better financial means, they can repay the reverse mortgage at any time and retain the property free of charge.
